nativeRange.habitat: Forested lowland streams and sluggish tributaries in the lower reaches of river systems, where the water is typically tannin-stained, very soft, and acidic. Dense leaf litter covers the substrate and overhanging vegetation shades the surface.

decorPreferences: heavily planted with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, hornwort, Cabomba)
driftwood and dried leaf litter for blackwater biotope effect

diet.notes: Feeds on small invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium, accepts high-quality flake, micro-pellets, and granules as a staple; supplement regularly with small live and frozen foods such as daphnia, brine shrimp, micro-worms, white mosquito larvae, and bloodworms. Varied diet maintains color vibrancy and conditioning for breeding.

goodWith: other small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, cardinal tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
dwarf cichlids (apistogramma species) in larger tanks
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving species that will outcompete for food
very small nano fish that may be intimidated

imageCandidates.notes: Category page containing ~12 images of H. rosaceus including live male specimens photographed at Wikicon Wuppertal 2019 (6000×4000 px). Reviewer should select a specific file from the category and upload to R2.

summary: The Rosy Tetra is a small, elegant Characin from the tannin-stained lowland streams of Guyana, Suriname, and Brazil. Its translucent pinkish body, blood-red fins, and iridescent white-tipped dorsal fin make it a standout community fish. Best kept in soft, acidic water in groups of six or more.
careNotes:
The Rosy Tetra (Hyphessobrycon rosaceus) inhabits forested blackwater tributaries in the Rio Essequibo, Rio Corantijn, and Rio Suriname drainage systems of Guyana, Suriname, and Brazil. It is closely related to — and frequently confused with — the Ornate Tetra (H. bentosi) and the Bentos Tetra (H. bentosi ornatus). The most reliable distinguishing feature is the absence in H. rosaceus of the dark marking just behind the operculum (the bony gill cover) that is present in H. bentosi. A further look-alike is H. roseus (Yellow Phantom Tetra), which has a yellower body tone. The vast majority of fish sold in the trade are commercially farm-bred rather than wild-caught.

This species performs best in soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–7.0, gH 1–10) with good filtration but gentle flow. Dark substrate and tannin-staining from driftwood or Indian almond leaves will both reduce stress and intensify coloration. Subdued lighting and a heavily planted tank help the fish feel secure and display more confidently. The species is generally peaceful but tends to be timid — avoid pairing it with boisterous or much larger tankmates. Keep in groups of at least 6, preferably 10 or more, as smaller groups tend to produce subdued fish that hide frequently. Males develop noticeably extended dorsal and anal fins as they mature and become slimmer and more colourful than females.
breedingNotes: The Rosy Tetra is considered moderately challenging to breed. Conditioning a pair or small group separately on live and frozen foods for one to two weeks before the attempt helps bring the fish into spawning condition. Transfer them to a dedicated breeding tank — approximately 45 cm × 25 cm × 25 cm — filled with very soft, acidic water (gH 1–5, pH 5.5–6.5) at 26–29°C. Fine-leaved plants or a mesh spawning grid placed just above the substrate will catch falling eggs and protect them from egg-eating adults. The Rosy Tetra is an egg scatterer, distributing eggs among vegetation; these hatch within 24–36 hours. Remove the adults once spawning has been observed. The fry become free-swimming 3–5 days after hatching and should be started on infusoria or commercial liquid fry food before graduating to brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Fry are sensitive to declining water quality, so small, frequent water changes with closely matched parameters are important during this stage.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hyphessobrycon rosaceus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rio Essequibo, Rio Corantijn, Rio Suriname basins; Guyana, Suriname, Brazil), adult size (4 cm SL), temperature (24–28°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (1–12°H), minimum tank dimensions (60×37.5×30 cm, ~70 L), diet, breeding water parameters (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5, 27–29°C), egg hatch time (24–36 h), look-alike distinction from H. bentosi (opercular spot), and trade note (most specimens farm-bred).

additional.name: FishBase — Hyphessobrycon rosaceus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3.4 cm SL maximum), temperature (24–28°C), pH (5.8–7.5), hardness (up to 20 dH), native range (Essequibo, Corantijn, and Suriname River basins), taxonomy (Characidae, Characiformes), trophic level 3.1 (omnivore), IUCN status Least Concern. FishBase max size 3.4 cm SL vs SeriouslyFish 4 cm SL — used 3.5–5.0 cm total length range to accommodate both measurements and trade-typical sizes.

additional.name: Aquadiction — Rosy Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming adult size (~5 cm), lifespan (up to 5 years), temperature (24–28°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (1–12 GH), diet (flakes, micropellets, bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia), minimum group size (6+), and breeding notes (soft acidic water, 26–29°C, eggs hatch 24–36 h, fry free-swim within 5 days, start on infusoria).

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Rosy Tetra
additional.notes: Additional confirmation of adult size (5 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (up to 20 dH), minimum group of 6 (preferably 10+), and look-alike note: Ornate Tetra (H. bentosi) distinguished by opercular spot absent in H. rosaceus.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer. Condition a pair or small group on live and frozen foods for one to two weeks, then transfer to a soft, acidic spawning tank (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5, 26–29°C) furnished with fine-leaved plants or a spawning mesh. Eggs hatch in 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming within 3–5 days. Remove adults once spawning is observed and start fry on infusoria, progressing to brine shrimp nauplii as they develop.
